content.text: Blameless Postmortem Template and Timeline Construction

A postmortem is not a report about what went wrong—it is a structured learning artifact that explains why a complex system produced a failure, what the organization can learn from it, and what will be changed to prevent recurrence or respond faster. The blameless discipline is not about removing accountability; it is about directing accountability at systems, processes, and procedures rather than at individuals, who acted with the information they had at the time.
Core Concept

The blameless postmortem was codified by Google's SRE culture and has since been adopted as the standard in high-reliability engineering organizations. Its core claim: in complex systems, nearly every serious incident involves multiple contributing factors—not a single person's mistake. When postmortems blame individuals, the result is defensiveness, information hiding, and organizational learning failure. When postmortems analyze system factors, the result is shared understanding and durable fixes.
The two-part postmortem contract:
the organization commits to not punishing people who acted in good faith with the information they had; engineers commit to full transparency about what happened and what they could see at the time.
Architecture: Postmortem Structure

attrs.alt: Blameless postmortem structure showing six sections: impact, timeline, root cause, detection, resolution, action items
attrs.title: Blameless Postmortem Structure

content.text: Section 1: Impact
The impact section provides the factual measurements of what the incident caused. It must be specific and measurable—no vague statements like 'some users were affected.'
Duration:
start time to resolution time, in minutes and hours. Include the time to first user impact if it differs from the alert trigger time.
Users affected:
percentage of active users impacted, or absolute number if the population is known. Break down by geography, tier, or feature if the impact was not uniform.
Error volume:
number of failed requests, token generation failures, or quality degradations. Distinguish between fully failed requests and degraded-quality responses.
SLA impact:
whether the incident triggered SLA breaches, the SLO consumed, and the remaining error budget.
Revenue impact:
if calculable, include estimated revenue impact. For GenAI API products with per-token pricing, failed requests translate directly to unrealized revenue and potential credit obligations.
Section 2: Timeline Construction

The timeline is the most valuable section of the postmortem. It reconstructs the sequence of events from the first system anomaly through to full resolution. A well-constructed timeline reveals where the gap between actual failure and detection was, which decisions extended or shortened the outage, and which escalation steps added delay.
Timeline Construction Principles
Use UTC throughout.
Mixed timezones in a timeline cause miscalculation and confusion during cross-timezone review.
Interleave system events and human actions.
'The deploy happened, then the error rate spiked, then the alert fired, then the engineer acknowledged' is the complete causal chain—not just the system events and not just the human actions.
Mark the five anchor events.

(1) First user impact, (2) first system alert, (3) human detection (may differ from alert), (4) IC declared, (5) mitigation applied, (6) resolution confirmed. These anchor events allow MTTD, MTTA, and MTTR to be measured.
Note confidence level.
When a timestamp is approximate rather than from a log, mark it: '[~14:32 UTC estimated from git commit timestamp]'. Approximate timestamps are better than no timestamps.

## Timeline (all times UTC)

14:28 — [System] Deploy of prompt-service v2.4.1 completed (automated)
14:28 — [System] LLM inference latency begins rising in us-east-1 (p99: 2.1s → 8.4s)
14:31 — [System] Error rate alert fires (threshold: 5% 5xx over 5min) — SEV-2 auto-declared
14:32 — [Engineer A] Acknowledges alert
14:34 — [Engineer A] Opens dashboard; observes latency spike starting at 14:28
14:35 — [Engineer A] Escalates to SEV-1; IC declared
14:36 — [War Room] Bridge call opens; Engineer B (infra) and C (prompt-service) join
14:40 — [Engineer C] Identifies deploy at 14:28 as temporal correlate
14:45 — [Engineer C] Proposes rollback; IC approves
14:47 — [Engineer C] Rollback initiated for prompt-service v2.4.1 → v2.4.0
14:52 — [System] Latency begins recovering
14:55 — [System] Error rate returns below 1%
14:58 — [IC] Confirms metrics stable; incident resolved

Anchor events:
- First user impact: 14:28 (coincides with deploy)
- First system detection: 14:31 (alert)
- Human detection: 14:32 (engineer acknowledges)
- IC declared: 14:35
- Mitigation: 14:47 (rollback initiated)
- Resolution: 14:58

MTTD: 3 min (14:28 to 14:31 alert)
MTTA: 4 min (14:28 to 14:32 acknowledgment)
MTTR: 30 min (14:28 to 14:58)
Section 3: Root Cause Analysis
Root cause analysis in a blameless postmortem describes why the system state led to the incident—not who caused it. The analysis has three layers:
Proximate cause.

The direct technical trigger: what changed or failed to cause the incident. 'A prompt template change removed the output format instruction, causing the parser downstream to fail on 30% of responses.'
Contributing factors.

System properties that allowed the proximate cause to produce an outage: 'No output format validation existed in the parser. The staging environment eval suite did not cover the affected prompt format. Alerts were tuned for infrastructure errors, not application-layer parsing failures.'
Safeguard gap.
Why the safeguards that should have caught this did not: 'The eval suite coverage for prompt format variants was 67%. The 33% gap included the exact format modified in this change.'

Notice that these three layers describe system properties, not individual errors. The prompt template change was made by someone—but the postmortem does not say 'Engineer X made a mistake.' It says 'the system lacked validation that would have caught this class of change.'
Section 6: Action Items

Action items are the entire output of the postmortem process. Everything else—the timeline, the root cause analysis—exists to produce well-reasoned action items. An action item without an owner, a target date, and a ticket link is an intention, not a commitment.
Category
Example
Priority Signal
Prevent
Add output format schema validation to prompt CI pipeline
Highest — eliminates class of failure
Detect sooner
Add parsing failure rate alert with 1% threshold (currently 5%)
High — reduces MTTD
Reduce MTTR
Add rollback automation for prompt-service to < 2 min (currently 8 min)
High — limits future impact duration
Improve runbooks
Document prompt rollback procedure in on-call runbook
Medium — reduces cognitive load
Postmortem Template

# Postmortem: [Short Title]
**Incident ID:** [INC-YYYYMMDD-NNN]
**Date:** [YYYY-MM-DD]
**Severity:** SEV-[N]
**Status:** Draft | In Review | Published
**Owner:** [Name — responsible for completing and publishing]
**Reviewers:** [List]

---

## Impact
- **Duration:** [X min] ([start_time]–[resolution_time] UTC)
- **Users affected:** [X% of active users] / [absolute number]
- **Error volume:** [N failed requests or degraded responses]
- **SLA impact:** [breach yes/no; error budget consumed]
- **Revenue impact:** [$X estimated] / [N credits owed]

---

## Timeline (all times UTC)
| Time | Event | Source |
|------|-------|--------|
| HH:MM | [event description] | System/[Name] |

**Anchor events:**
- First user impact: [time]
- First system alert: [time]
- Human detection: [time]
- IC declared: [time]
- Mitigation: [time]
- Resolution: [time]

MTTD: [X min] | MTTA: [X min] | MTTR: [X min]

---

## Root Cause
**Proximate cause:** [Technical trigger]
**Contributing factors:**
- [Factor 1: system property that allowed this to cause an outage]
- [Factor 2]
**Safeguard gap:** [Why existing safeguards did not prevent or catch this]

---

## What Went Well
- [Aspect of the response that worked effectively]

## What Could Be Improved
- [Aspect of the response that added unnecessary delay or confusion]

---

## Action Items
| # | Description | Category | Owner | Target | Ticket |
|---|-------------|----------|-------|--------|--------|
| 1 | [action] | Prevent | [name] | [date] | [link] |

---

*This postmortem is blameless. It describes system factors, not individual fault.
All participants are thanked for their transparency in contributing to this analysis.*
GenAI-Specific Postmortem Considerations
Output quality incidents need eval evidence.
For incidents involving degraded output quality, the root cause section must include the eval suite results that confirmed the degradation and validated the fix. Anecdotal examples are insufficient.
Prompt changes require version evidence.
Link to the specific commit or prompt registry version that changed. The timeline should show the exact prompt template diff as a contributing factor, not just 'a prompt was modified.'
Model version changes require provider context.

If the incident was triggered by a silent model API change from the LLM provider, include communication from the provider in the contributing factors and add a detection action item for provider changelog monitoring.
Safety incidents require separate review track.
Incidents involving harmful AI outputs go through a safety review process in addition to the standard postmortem. The postmortem does not replace the safety review; they run in parallel.
The What Went Well Section

Most postmortem templates include a 'What Went Well' section, and most teams fill it in perfunctorily or skip it. This is a mistake. The 'What Went Well' section serves a specific purpose: it identifies the response behaviors and system properties that limited the damage and should be preserved and replicated.
Candidates for What Went Well:
The circuit breaker triggered correctly and limited the blast radius to one region.
The on-call engineer identified the deploy correlation within 6 minutes of joining the bridge call.
The rollback procedure worked on the first attempt without complications.
The war room timeline in the incident doc was kept current throughout, making the postmortem reconstruction straightforward.
The status page update was posted within 4 minutes of incident declaration.

These observations do not require action items. They require recognition and, where appropriate, formalization: 'the circuit breaker behavior that worked well here should be documented and replicated in all API gateway services.'
Postmortem Meeting Facilitation

The postmortem meeting is not a debrief. It is a structured review of a written document, aimed at improving the analysis and refining the action items. The document should be at least 80% complete before the meeting.
Meeting Agenda (60-minute format)
0-5 min: Framing.
Facilitator reminds attendees of the blameless norm. No fault attribution. Purpose is learning and improvement.
5-20 min: Timeline walk-through.
Postmortem owner walks through the timeline section. Attendees who were present correct and fill in gaps.
20-35 min: Root cause discussion.
Facilitator leads 5 Whys or similar analysis to ensure the contributing factors are complete and the safeguard gap is clearly understood.
35-55 min: Action item review and assignment.
Review each action item for completeness: does it have an owner, a target date, and a ticket link? Assign owners in the room if unassigned.
55-60 min: Publication plan.
Confirm who will finalize and publish by what date. For SEV-1, this must be within 5 business days.
The 5 Whys Applied to GenAI Incidents

The 5 Whys is a structured root cause technique: ask 'why did this happen?' repeatedly until you reach a systemic root cause rather than a proximate one. For GenAI systems, each 'why' should point toward a system property, not an individual action.

Incident: LLM API returned malformed JSON for 28% of requests over 45 minutes

Why 1: Why did the API return malformed JSON?
→ The response parser received raw model output that lacked the expected JSON wrapper.

Why 2: Why was the JSON wrapper missing from model output?
→ A prompt template change removed the explicit 'respond only in valid JSON' instruction.

Why 3: Why was the prompt change deployed without catching this?
→ The eval suite for the affected prompt path covered only 4 of 12 output format variants.

Why 4: Why did the eval suite cover only 4 of 12 variants?
→ Eval coverage was added manually when new variants were created; no automated coverage enforcement existed.

Why 5: Why was there no automated coverage enforcement?
→ The prompt eval framework was built to measure quality scores, not to enforce coverage of output format variants. No tooling existed to generate a coverage report.

Systemic root cause: Prompt CI lacks output format coverage measurement and enforcement.
Action item: Build eval coverage reporter that enforces 100% output format variant coverage before any prompt PR merges.
Postmortem Automation and Tooling

Creating the first draft of a postmortem from incident data can be partially automated. War room channel history, timeline entries, and action items from the incident management system can be assembled into a structured draft:
Postmortem Quality Criteria
Before publishing, reviewers should evaluate a postmortem against these quality criteria:
Timeline has all anchor events with timestamps.
MTTD, MTTA, and MTTR can be calculated directly from the timeline.
Root cause reaches a systemic level.
'5 Whys complete' — the root cause is a system property, not 'someone made a change.'
No blame language.
Replace 'Engineer X forgot to' with 'the system had no automated check for.' Replace 'the team failed to' with 'the process did not include.'
All action items have owner, date, and ticket.
No action item without all three fields is allowed in a published postmortem.
Impact is quantified.
No vague language like 'significant impact' or 'many users affected.' Numbers or percentages required.
Key Takeaways

The timeline is the most valuable section. A carefully reconstructed timeline with anchor events enables MTTD, MTTA, and MTTR measurement and reveals exactly where the incident could have been shortened.
Blameless means system-focused, not accountability-free. Engineers are still responsible for action items; the postmortem simply doesn't attribute the incident to individual fault.
Action items need four fields: what, who, when, and a ticket link. An action item without all four is an intention, not a commitment that can be tracked.
Template the postmortem before the first incident. Filling a template under time pressure is manageable; designing a structure while also running a postmortem meeting is not.
